question.text: What restrictions apply to insurance companies participating in interest rate derivatives?

answer.text: Insurance companies are allowed to use IRD only for hedging purposes (long hedge)
Insurance companies are prohibited from all IRD participation
Insurance companies can trade IRD without any restrictions
Insurance companies can only trade through Primary Dealers

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Insurance companies are allowed to participate in IRD but only for hedging purposes - specifically long hedges to protect their G-Sec portfolios. They cannot take speculative positions or short hedges.
**The Trap:** They are NOT prohibited entirely - hedging is allowed. They DO face restrictions (hedging only). They can trade through any registered broker, not just PDs.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Different institutional participants have different usage restrictions. MFs can hedge too. Banks have fewer restrictions. Understanding these distinctions is key for Series IV.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Insurance companies hold massive G-Sec portfolios to match their long-term liabilities. IRD hedging helps them manage interest rate risk on these holdings without selling the bonds.
